title: Stratospheric ozone concentration projections
subtitle: Stratospheric ozone concentrations with projections to 2100 based on chemistry-climate models. These are measured as global and regional averages relative to levels in 1960 (1960 = 0).
caption:
Stratospheric ozone concentration projections, 1960 to 2100
Stratospheric ozone concentrations with projections to 2100 based on chemistry-climate models. These are measured as global and regional averages relative to levels in 1960 (1960 = 0).

The line chart tracks modeled changes in stratospheric ozone concentrations from 1960 through 2100, measuring global and regional averages relative to the baseline level established in 1960. The vertical axis spans from -140 to +40, representing the percentage deviation from the 1960 baseline, while the horizontal axis covers the 140-year period. Three distinct trajectories appear: the Arctic region, the Global average, and the Antarctic region. The Arctic line begins at zero in 1960, rises slightly to around 5 by 1970, then declines to approximately -25 by 2000 before reversing direction and climbing steadily to reach nearly 40 by 2100. The Global average line follows a similar but more moderate path, dipping to roughly -15 around 2000 and recovering to just above zero by the end of the century. The Antarctic line shows the most extreme variation, plunging from zero in 1960 to a minimum near -120 around 2005, then ascending consistently through the remainder of the projection period to finish near -5 by 2100. All three lines converge near the zero baseline in the early decades, diverge significantly through the late twentieth century, and show recovery trends in the twenty-first century. The Antarctic region displays the largest magnitude of change throughout the entire period, with its lowest point occurring around 2005 and its recovery trajectory extending across the final decades of the projection.
